prompt: Two cache adapters implement the exact same `get`/`set` signatures, yet swapping one for the other breaks callers in production. Which explanations are plausible? (Select all that apply.)
choices: One is per-instance isolated while the other is shared across instances, changing consistency
One `get` can never fail while the other can time out, and callers had no error handling
One enforces a maximum value size the other accepts, so some writes now get rejected
Matching method signatures guarantee identical behaviour, so the cause must be unrelated

prompt: A dependency is a mature, standardised protocol you will never replace, has exactly one implementation, and no need to be faked in tests. What does introducing a swappability interface around it most likely represent?
choices: Speculative generality — ongoing indirection cost with no realistic payoff
A required step before any code can be tested
The only way to keep callers decoupled from the protocol

prompt: You abstract over several providers and discover the contract can only expose features ALL of them support, so you lose access to the one provider's standout capability you actually need. This is the lowest-common-denominator trap. What are reasonable responses? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Decide swappability isn't worth it here and commit directly to the provider with the needed feature
Keep the generic contract but widen it, accepting that weaker adapters must emulate or reject the feature
Bolt a hidden vendor-specific escape hatch onto the 'generic' contract and tell no one
Recognise that breadth of provider support trades against depth of capability, and choose deliberately
